questions.q: Identify the function of the Domain Name System (DNS) as described in the episode.
options: It translates a human-friendly website name into the numerical address machines actually use to connect
It encrypts the connection between a browser and a website so traffic cannot be read
It stores the website's files and serves the pages back to the user's browser
It distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers to prevent overload
questions.explanation: The episode calls DNS 'the phone book of the internet': computers talk to numbers, not names, so DNS translates a name into the numerical address behind it before any connection can be made. Encryption is a separate concern, and DNS does not host or serve the site's actual pages — Twitter's own servers were fine; only the lookups failed.

questions.q: Why could a flood of traffic aimed at one company, Dyn, make hundreds of unrelated websites unreachable at once?
options: The attack deleted the website files stored on Twitter, Netflix and the other affected services
Dyn provided the DNS for a huge fraction of popular sites, so knocking out Dyn meant users could no longer look up those sites' addresses
Dyn hosted the actual web servers for all of those companies in a single data centre
The attack spread a virus from Dyn's servers to every connected website
questions.explanation: Dyn was the shared DNS provider for a huge slice of the web's most-used services, so flooding Dyn meant devices could not get the address for any of them — the sites' own servers kept running perfectly. The trap (option C) confuses DNS lookup with web hosting: Dyn was the phone book, not the host. As the episode puts it, people could not look up the number, so they could not make the call.

questions.q: The episode unpacks the name 'distributed denial-of-service' word by word. What does each part of the name tell you about the attack?
options: 'Distributed' means the data was spread across many servers; 'denial-of-service' means the service refused to load due to a software bug
'Distributed' means the attack was shared between several hackers; 'denial-of-service' means the company stopped offering the service
'Denial-of-service' means legitimate users are denied access (availability is removed); 'distributed' means the flood comes from a vast, scattered army of machines rather than one blockable source
'Denial-of-service' means data was stolen and held for ransom; 'distributed' means the ransom was spread across many victims
questions.explanation: The episode breaks the term apart: the goal of denial-of-service is to deny legitimate users the service by making it unavailable, and 'distributed' means the flood comes from many scattered machines at once, so you cannot just block a single source. A tempting wrong answer confuses 'distributed' with multiple attackers, but no data theft or ransom is involved — this attack stole nothing.

questions.q: Identify what the attackers used to generate the enormous flood of traffic against Dyn.
options: Thousands of high-powered servers rented from cloud data centres
A single supercomputer capable of sending over a terabit of traffic per second
Malicious code injected directly into Dyn's own DNS servers
A botnet of ordinary hijacked IoT devices such as webcams, baby monitors, routers and DVRs
questions.explanation: The army was made of ordinary internet-connected devices — webcams, baby monitors, home routers and DVRs — that had been silently hijacked into a botnet, a network of infected machines controlled remotely. The whole point of the story is that they did not buy servers or hack data centres; the weapon was the cheap, forgotten gadgets in people's homes.

questions.q: Explain how the Mirai software was able to take over hundreds of thousands of devices without using any sophisticated exploit.
options: It broke the encryption protecting each device using brute-force computing power
It exploited an unpatched bug in the devices' operating systems
It tried a short list of common factory-default usernames and passwords, and on devices where the default had never been changed the door simply swung open
It intercepted and stole the passwords as owners typed them into their devices
questions.explanation: The episode stresses that Mirai was 'almost insultingly simple' — it just guessed passwords, trying a list of around sixty common factory defaults like 'admin/admin' and 'root/root'. Because manufacturers ship identical defaults and almost nobody changes them, device after device let Mirai in. It broke no encryption and used no clever exploit (options A and B), which is exactly what makes the lesson so pointed.

questions.q: Of the three pillars of information security, which one was the target of the Dyn attack, and why?
options: Confidentiality, because the attack read and leaked users' private data
Integrity, because the attack altered the contents of the affected websites
Authentication, because the attack stole users' login credentials from Dyn
Availability, because the attack made services unreachable for legitimate users without stealing or changing any data
questions.explanation: The episode names this as the textbook picture of an availability attack: it stole nothing, read no data and broke no encryption — it simply made Dyn unreachable so legitimate users could not get to the services they needed. A system can be perfectly confidential and perfectly intact yet useless if nobody can reach it, which is why a denial-of-service attack goes straight for that third pillar, availability.

questions.q: Explain why describing Dyn as a 'single point of failure' captures the structural lesson of the attack.
options: Dyn was the only company in the world capable of running DNS, so there was no alternative
So many unrelated sites depended on the same one DNS provider that one component failing took a huge amount down with it — a concentration of risk in a single fragile link
Dyn's servers were located in a single building, so a power cut would have had the same effect
Dyn used a single password for all of its DNS servers, which the attackers guessed
questions.explanation: A single point of failure is a concentration of risk where one component, if it goes down, takes a huge amount with it — exactly the case when hundreds of sites all relied on Dyn for DNS. Option C is too narrow (the issue was shared dependency, not physical location), and option A overstates it: other DNS providers existed, which is precisely why the fix was to spread the dependency across more than one provider for redundancy.

questions.q: Distinguish between who was careless and who actually paid the price in the Dyn attack, and explain what principle this illustrates.
options: The owners of hijacked devices lost nothing, while Twitter, Netflix and ordinary users paid the price — showing that an insecure device endangers strangers, not just its owner
The webcam owners were careless and they were the ones who lost their data and devices
Dyn was careless with its passwords, so Dyn alone suffered the consequences
The website companies were careless about redundancy, so only they were harmed while device owners were unaffected
questions.explanation: The episode highlights a sharp twist on responsibility: the owner of a hijacked webcam lost nothing — their camera kept working — while the harm fell on Twitter, Netflix and everyone trying to use them. The principle is that insecure devices are not just a private risk; they become ammunition conscripted into harming others, so security is not purely a personal matter.

questions.q: A manufacturer designs a new internet-connected security camera and wants to prevent it being conscripted into a botnet like Mirai's. Recommend the most effective design choice and justify it.
options: Print a different random default password on a sticker on each unit, and ship it ready to use
Add stronger encryption to the video stream so footage cannot be intercepted
Make the device refuse to operate until the owner sets a new, strong password, so the secure choice is also the default and easy choice
Speed up the device's processor so it can detect and block malicious login attempts
questions.explanation: Mirai succeeded because of insecure defaults and weak authentication, so the fix is to make the secure choice the default and the easy choice: a device that refuses to operate until a real password is set, as in the episode's 'DeviceFirstBoot' design. Encryption protects confidentiality but does nothing about the guessable login that let Mirai in, and a per-unit sticker password is weaker because it still ships in a usable state with a credential the owner may never change.

questions.q: A streaming company that went dark during the Dyn outage asks how to stop one provider failure from taking its whole service offline again. Applying the episode's structural lesson, what should you recommend?
options: Move all of its DNS to a single larger, more reliable provider with stronger servers
Encrypt all of its DNS traffic so attackers cannot read the lookup requests
Force all of its users to set strong passwords on their home devices before connecting
Use more than one DNS provider so that if the primary is unreachable a secondary can still resolve names, removing the single point of failure
questions.explanation: The structural lesson is the opposite of concentration: spread the dependency and build in redundancy so no single failure can cascade, which is why many affected sites adopted multiple DNS providers after the attack. Moving to one bigger provider just relocates the single point of failure, and forcing users to set strong device passwords, while good security advice in general, does not address this company's own availability risk — the question to keep asking is what one thing, if it fails, takes everything with it.
